creator: edwin@mavetju.org
text:
On Wed, Sep 26, 2007 at 07:45:18PM -0400, Joe Marcus Clarke wrote:
> Edwin Groothuis wrote:
> > The following reply was made to PR ports/116218; it has been noted by GNATS.
> > 
> > From: Edwin Groothuis <edwin@mavetju.org>
> > To: "Jason E. Hale" <bsdkaffee@gmail.com>
> > Cc: bug-followup@freebsd.org
> > Subject: Re: ports/116218: ports-mgmt/portlint - IFF -> IF
> > Date: Thu, 27 Sep 2007 09:28:43 +1000
> > 
> >  On Wed, Sep 26, 2007 at 07:23:01PM -0400, Jason E. Hale wrote:
> >  > I used portlint today and noticed the same string.  It brought me back to
> >  > calculus class.  The capital letters gave it away.
> >  > IFF is used in logic to mean "if and only if".  If I am not mistaken, that is
> >  > the intended use here.  See: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_and_only_if
> >  > Perhaps more than most porters need be familiar with.
> >  
> >  Very understandable if you're using calculus. I'm not using calculus
> >  here, and the rest of the line doesn't make it clear that is what
> >  is meant.
> >  
> >  If I don't get it, I doubt there are much others who will get it.
> 
> Actually, it's from geometric proofs, and it is intended to mean If and
> only If.  I don't really see the need to change it.  We are all geeks
> after all.  A few geeky things doesn't really hurt anything.

Keep in mind that this is an specific (US-)English math-jargon
thing, and that there are a lot of people outside whose primary
language isn't English, nor who are math-wizzies, nor who understand
what is meant with "If and only if".

I for one, with a technical study and full calculus and geometry
courses at high school, don't understand how it is different from
a normal "if".

Edwin
